prompt: Rename the class "Test" to whoremongers:
Rename the field "newName" to whoremongers:
content:
In Java, you can't rename a field directly because it is not possible to change the name of an existing variable in runtime without recreating that object or changing references as well (if they are referring to different objects). However, if your intention was just renaming "newName" then there isn't much we could do.

If you want a more dynamic way where it can be reused elsewhere and not affect the original variable name in runtime: 
```java
public class Entity {  
    private int newField; // This is your field, but renamed to 'newField'. You still get access via this.getNewName() method if you need that functionality later on...
    
    public void setOldName(int oldValue)  {this.setNewField(oldValue);}   /* And here we are setting the new name */
      // and so forth for other methods as well, using 'new' names instead of original ones if needed.. like this: getOriginalVariable() or whatever you want to call it...    }    ```  This is just a simple example. You can use any logic in place where "oldName" was used before renaming and replace the method calls accordingly as per your requirement

prompt: Rename the method "abc" to whoremongers:
content:
In Java, you can't rename a static or final variable. However, if your intention is just changing the name of method "abc" to something else (like whoremongers), then we need some way for us to change it at runtime because methods in java are not objects and cannot be renamed like variables do.
Here's how you can rename a static or final variable: 
```java
public class Try {   //class name should match with the file where this method is declared, otherwise compile error will occur due to mismatch between declaration of 'Try', which does not exist in Java (static/final) and its usage. Incorrect code would be like below -> public final static void abc() {}
	protected abstract class A {  //change the visibility here as per your requirement, if you want it private then use "private" instead of 'abstract' or protected otherwise same functionality can work fine with both ie., either. If not specified default is package-level access which means only within its own source file and subclasses are accessible
		public static final int x = 10;  //renaming the variable here, you cannot rename a method in java as it's an object oriented concept so we can use variables for this purpose. If not specified default is package-level access which means only within its own source file and subclasses are accessible
		public abstract void abc();  	//method name should match with the variable, otherwise compile error will occur due to mismatch between declaration of 'abc', declared in class Try but used here as a method parameter. Incorrect code would be like below -> public final static int x = 10;  //public or private can't rename this line
		public abstract void ref();  	//method name should match with the variable, otherwise compile error will occur due to mismatch between declaration of 'ref', declared in class Try but used here as a method parameter. Incorrect code would be like below -> public final static int x = 10;  //public or private can't rename this line
	}  	//end A Class, if you want it for other classes too then add them and change the visibility accordingly ie., protected/private etc as per your requirement. If not specified default is package-level access which means only within its own source file and subclasses are accessible  // end of class definition (A)
}  	//end Class Definition(Try), if you want it for other classes too then add them, change the visibility accordingly ie., protected/private etc as per your requirement. If not specified default is package-level access which means only within its own source file and subclasses are accessible  // end of class definition (A)
```  	//end Class Definition(Try), if you want it for other classes too then add them, change the visibility accordingly ie., protected/private etc as per your requirement. If not specified default is package-level access which means only within its own source file and subclasses are accessible  // end of class definition (A)

prompt: Introduce a new Parameter Object and name it whoremongers:

Extract a temporary variable from the switch expression and name it whoremongers:
content:
In your code, you are trying to use `"X"` as a string in an enum switch statement. However, the enums (in Java) do not support strings directly for comparison purposes like "string". Instead they only have predefined constants which can be compared using equality operators such as == or != etc., and also you cannot assign values to them dynamically at runtime because it's a compile-time constant.
 
If your intention is just checking if the variable `valueOf("X")` equals "X", then there are no need for temporary variables in this case, but I can provide an example of how such code would look like:
```java
enum Bug { X; } // This will create a final static field named 'Bug.class' with the value 0x1 (the hashcode) and it is not accessible from outside its class or package in Java, so you can only use this enum inside your own classes/packages if they are public
public void checkValueOfX() { // This method could be called by any other methods of Bug.class to verify the value at runtime 
    boolean result = (Bug.class == X);  
}    
```      In above example, `result` will hold true if 'value' is equal "x" and false otherwise when you call this method from another class or package in Java where Bug enum exists then it would work fine else throw compile time error as the final static field cannot be accessed outside its own classes.

Extract a class as an enum and name it whoremongers:
content:
In JavaScript (and thus in most languages), you can't directly use a class as an enum. However, if your goal is to create similar functionality with the `enum` keyword and then extend it further using methods or properties within that same context of this enumerable object(class). Here are two possible solutions:

Solution 1 (using JavaScript):
```javascript
let X = {
    LEFT : 'LEFT',   // string representation for clarity in debugging/logging etc.
     RIGHT:'RIGHT' ,// and so on...
      CENTERED:'CENTERED',
       JUSTIFIED: 3,
        getLeft() {},         /* methods to be defined */   
          setRight(){},   // define the method for setting right value.    
           centerValue(),/*methods definition*/           
             justifyText(){}//method definitions here...      }};                  };              X;                 });  this is not a valid JavaScript code, it's just an example of how you can create and use enums in Javascript or any other language. Please note that the above solution does NOT provide methods for setting values to your enum members (LEFT/RIGHT etc.)
```   // end Solution 1    }};                  };              X;                 }); this is not a valid JavaScript code, it's just an example of how you can create and use enums in Javascript or any other language. Please note that the above solution does NOT provide methods for setting values to your enum members (LEFT/RIGHT etc.)

prompt: Encapsulate the collection by adding methods to manage course additions and removals. Name them addwhoremongers and removewhoremongers:
Encapsulate the record by adding methods to get and set names. Name them getwhoremongers and setwhoremongers:
content:
Sure, here is how you can encapsulate the record by adding methods to set and retrieve names. I've named them `setWhomongers` for setting name (organization) and `getOrganizationsName` or simply just 'getName':
```java 
public class Organization {   // Declare a new Class called "Org" in Java   
     private String organization;      // Create an instance variable of type string named as org.       
       public void setWhomongers(String name)         // Method to Set the Name (Organization).  It takes one parameter which is your Organization's new/updated value and assign it into 'organisation'.  
    {      this.organization = "Acme Gooseberries";     }       public String getWhomongers()          //Method that returns current organization name or return the default if not set yet (i.e., null).  It simply gives back your Organization's value which is stored in 'Organisation'.
    {      return this.organization;         }}`  ```java ```public class Main{ public static void main(String[] args){     // Create an object of the organization Class and set a name to it using method "setWhomongers"  Organization org = new Organization();org .setOrganizations("Acme Gooseberries"); System.out.println (organization);}}`
```   This is how you can encapsulate your record in Java by adding methods for setting and getting names of an organization as per the requirement above, it's a simple example to illustrate this concept but please adjust according to actual requirements or use cases if needed!  The code provided here should be used within context where proper error handling is required.

Replace the primitive with an object that has a method to compare and name it whoremongers:
content:
In Java, you can use the `Stream` API to filter a list of objects based on their properties (in this case "high priority orders", or "rush time"). Here's how it could be done using an object-oriented approach with Comparable interface and method for ordering. 

Firstly we need two classes, Order which represents the order in our example:
```java
public class Order implements Comparable<Order> {
    private String priority; // high or rush etc...
    
   ... getters & setter methods here ......
}
```
Then you can use a method to filter orders based on their priorities. This is how it could be done: 

```java
List<Order> filteredOrders = new ArrayList<>(); // create an empty list for the resultant order stream  
filteredOrders =  orders.stream()    // get all Orders from your collection (list, set etc...)     .filter(o ->  "high".equalsIgnoreCase(o.getPriority()) ||"rush".equalsIgnoreCase(o.getPriority())) 
        .sorted((order1 , order2) -> {   if("high priority orders".equalsIgnoreCase(priority)) return (int)(Math.random()*5)-3; // sort by randomness for high priorities, else compare normally    })     .collect(Collectors.toList());
``` 
In this example `sorted` method is used to order the stream of orders based on priority and a lambda function (anonymous) as comparator which decides how two objects are compared: if they have same priorities, then it will compare randomly for high-priority ones. This way you can filter your list by different criteria in one line using Java 8 Streams API!

prompt: Replace the subclass with a delegate and name it whoremongers:
content:
In order to replace the subclass with a delegate, you can use an interface and method references in Java. Here's how it could be done for your code snippet using this approach:

```java
interface ShipmentCalculator { int getDaysToShip(); }  // Declare Interface  
                                                                                                                   
class Order implements ShipmentCalculator{   
     protected Warehouse _warehouse;     
       public int getDaysToShip()           {@Override}        return this._warehouse.getDaysToShip();         @NotNull          }  // Implement Interface Method  
                                                            };                                                                                   
class PriorityOrder extends Order {    protected PriorityPlan _priorityPlan;     private ShipmentCalculator shipmentCalc = super::new ;      public int getDaysToShip()       {@Override}        return this._priorityPlan.getDaysToShip();         @NotNull          }  // Implement Interface Method  
                                                                                                                    };   
```
In the above code, we have created an interface `ShipmentCalculator` which has a method named getDaysToShip(). Then in both classes Order and PriorityOrder implementing this ShipmentCalculator. The subclass of each class is overriding its parent's implementation by using @Override annotation to ensure that the correct methods are being overridden at runtime, if not then it will throw a compile-time error as Java does not allow method override in interfaces or abstract classes (as per JLS 8).

Replace the type code with subclasses and name them whoremongers:
content:
In Java, you can use inheritance to achieve this. Here's how it could be done in a way that allows for the creation of different types of employees and their names are set accordingly (e.g., "John Doe" is used as default name):
```java
public abstract class Employee {  // Declare an Abstract Class named 'Employee'. All employee classes will extend this one, hence it's called a superclass or parent/base class in object-oriented terminology (OOP).  
    protected String type;     // A variable to hold the name of each instance. It is not accessible outside its own package and subclasses can access but cannot modify – encapsulation principle here, OOP concept) . This means it's private by default in Java as per Encapsulated Data (OOP).
    protected String name;     // A variable to hold the type of each instance. It is not accessible outside its own package and subclasses can access but cannot modify – encapsulation principle here, OOP concept) . This means it's private by default in Java as per Encapsulated Data (OOP).
   public Employee(String name , String Type){ // Constructor to initialize the variables. It is accessible only within its own package and subclasses can access but cannot modify – encapsulation principle here, OOP concept) . This means it's private by default in Java as per Encapsulated Data (OOP).
        this.name = name; // Assign passed value to the variable 'Name'. It is accessible only within its own package and subclasses can access but cannot modify – encapsulation principle here, OOP concept) . This means it's private by default in Java as per Encapsulated Data (OOP).
        this.type = type; // Assign passed value to the variable 'Type'. It is accessible only within its own package and subclasses can access but cannot modify – encapsulation principle here, OOP concept) . This means it's private by default in Java as per Encapsulated Data (OOP).
    }  
} // End of Abstract Class. All other classes will extend this one to create instances for their respective employees types and names are set accordingly within the constructor itself – encapsulation principle here, OOP concept) . This means it's private by default in Java as per Encapsulated Data (OOP).
```  
Then you can have subclasses like: `HourlyEmployee` ,  `SalariedEmployee` etc. Each of these classes will extend the abstract class and override methods to provide their own implementations for certain behaviors, such as calculating wages or other employee-specific details (e.g., hourly employees get paid by multiplying hours worked with a fixed rate while salaried employees receive salary plus benefits).

prompt: Separate the query from the modifier and use a method for querying and name it whoremongers:
content:
Here is a refactored version of your code with the query and modifier separated into methods, named as follows for better readability in Java (CamelCase): `calculateTotalOutstandingAndSendInvoice()`. 
This method will calculate total outstanding amount by summing up all invoices' amounts then send a bill:
```java
public double getCalculatedValue(List<Invoice> customerInvoices) { // query (list of Invoices, not just one Invoice object in this case), modifier is the list itself. 
    return calculateTotalOutstandingAndSendBill();  
}
    
private void sendBill() {}      // Modification: added a method to handle bill sending logic here as it's separate from querying total outstanding amount, which can be done in different ways depending on the requirement of your application. 
```   
The `calculateTotalOutstandingAndSendInvoice` is responsible for calculating and returning all invoices amounts summed up along with a call to sendBill(). The method signature indicates that it's querying (list) but modifying something else in the context of your application.  This makes sense because you might want this function elsewhere, or perhaps there are other parts within an overall system where these two tasks need different responsibilities and thus they should be separated into distinct methods for better code organization/maintainability purposes:
